name: Policies for information security
description: Requires the organization to establish a documented set of information security policies with formal management endorsement, distributed to staff and relevant external parties.
guidance: Policy content should be grounded in the organization's strategic objectives, applicable regulatory and legal obligations, and the current threat environment. A single umbrella policy may be sufficient for smaller or less complex organizations, with topic-specific documents added as scope demands.

name: Review of the policies for information security
description: Calls for the information security policy set to undergo periodic review and reassessment whenever significant organisational or environmental changes arise, confirming that policies remain fit for purpose.
guidance: Each policy should have a designated owner who holds approved management accountability for its lifecycle, including development and periodic evaluation. Review cycles should examine whether the organisation's changing circumstances — legal, technical, commercial, or operational — have introduced gaps or rendered existing policy positions inadequate.

name: Information security roles and responsibilities
description: Establishes that every information security responsibility across the organisation must be explicitly defined and assigned to named roles or individuals.
guidance: Role assignments should flow from the overarching information security policy. Both asset-level custodianship and process-level accountability need to be identified so that no responsibility falls through the gaps; auditors typically look for a documented RACI or equivalent allocation artefact.

name: Segregation of duties
description: Mandates the separation of incompatible duties and areas of accountability to limit exposure to unauthorised or accidental changes and misuse of organisational assets.
guidance: No single individual should be in a position to access, alter, or exploit assets without detection by another party. Particular attention should be given to separating the initiation of a transaction or action from the authority to approve it.

name: Contact with authorities
description: Obliges the organisation to establish and sustain working relationships with relevant external authorities, including law enforcement and regulatory bodies.
guidance: Documented procedures should specify the circumstances under which external bodies are to be contacted, who within the organisation carries that responsibility, and how security incidents are escalated to those bodies in a timely manner.

name: Contact with special interest groups
description: Addresses the need to cultivate ongoing engagement with specialist security communities, forums, and professional associations where appropriate.
guidance: Participation in such groups offers a practical channel for keeping pace with emerging threats, evolving best practices, and sector-specific guidance that may not yet be reflected in formal standards.

name: Information security in project management
description: Requires that information security considerations be embedded within project governance structures, irrespective of the project type or delivery methodology in use.
guidance: Integrating security into the organisation's project management approach ensures that information security risks surface during initiation and planning phases rather than after deployment. Auditors look for evidence that security risk assessments are a mandatory gate in the project lifecycle.

name: Mobile device policy
description: Calls for a dedicated policy, supported by commensurate technical and procedural safeguards, to govern the use of mobile devices and manage the associated information security risks.
guidance: Mobile device governance should specifically account for the heightened exposure arising from use in uncontrolled environments such as public networks and shared spaces. The policy should address device registration, acceptable use, data minimisation, and what happens when a device is lost or stolen.

name: Teleworking
description: Establishes that a policy and appropriate supporting controls must be in place to safeguard information that is accessed, processed, or stored at locations outside the organisation's main premises.
guidance: Remote working arrangements should receive explicit authorisation only when adequate security measures are confirmed as operational and aligned with organisational policy. The review should consider both the suitability of the remote environment and the sensitivity of the information involved.

name: Screening
description: Requires background verification checks on employment candidates to be conducted in a manner consistent with applicable law, ethical standards, and proportionate to the sensitivity of the role and the information it involves.
guidance: Checks should respect privacy and data protection legislation at every stage. Where legally permitted, verification may encompass identity confirmation, academic and professional credential validation, and independent reference checks. The depth of screening should scale with the classification level of information the candidate will access.

name: Terms and conditions of employment
description: Obliges the organisation to ensure that employment and contractor agreements clearly articulate security obligations for both the individual and the organisation.
guidance: Contractual terms should mirror and reinforce the organisation's information security policies. Importantly, certain obligations — such as confidentiality and data handling duties — should be drafted to survive the termination of the engagement.

name: Management responsibilities
description: Mandates that management actively requires employees and contractors to uphold information security standards consistent with established organisational policy and procedure.
guidance: Managerial accountability extends beyond issuing policy; it encompasses fostering awareness among personnel of their security duties and maintaining an environment in which those duties are understood and taken seriously.

name: Information security awareness, education and training
description: Calls for all personnel — and contractors where relevant — to receive security awareness education and training that is appropriate to their function, with content kept current through regular updates on policies and procedures.
guidance: Awareness programmes should be introduced at the point of onboarding and maintained as a continuous activity throughout employment. Coverage should evolve in step with changes to policy, the threat landscape, and the individual's responsibilities.

name: Disciplinary process
description: Establishes that a formal, documented disciplinary mechanism must exist and be communicated to staff, enabling the organisation to take proportionate action when an information security breach is confirmed.
guidance: The process should not be initiated until the occurrence of a breach has been substantiated. Responses should be graduated, weighing the seriousness and impact of the incident, the individual's intent, and any mitigating circumstances before determining an appropriate course of action.

name: Termination or change of employment responsibilities
description: Addresses the need to identify, communicate, and enforce information security obligations that persist beyond the end of an employment relationship or role change.
guidance: Exit and transition communications should cover any ongoing legal duties and, where applicable, continuing confidentiality commitments arising from employment terms. Contractors and employees in changed roles should receive explicit notice of which obligations remain in force and for how long.

name: Inventory of assets
description: Requires the organisation to identify all assets associated with information and information processing facilities and to compile and maintain a current inventory of those assets.
guidance: The inventory should capture details sufficient to support recovery in a disruption scenario, including asset type, format, physical or logical location, backup status, licensing details, and relative business value. An accurate register is a prerequisite for effective risk treatment and is commonly the first artefact an auditor requests.

name: Ownership of assets
description: Establishes that every asset recorded in the inventory must have an identified owner who carries approved accountability for its management and security.
guidance: Ownership may rest with an individual or a team, but in either case the owner must hold documented responsibility for decisions regarding the asset's production, development, maintenance, use, and protection. Unowned assets are a common audit finding.

name: Acceptable use of assets
description: Mandates that rules governing acceptable use of information assets and related processing facilities be documented and actively enforced.
guidance: Personnel and third-party users who interact with organisational assets must be made familiar with the applicable security requirements. Acceptable use guidance should be tailored to the type of asset and the sensitivity of information involved.

name: Return of assets
description: Obliges all employees and external party users to surrender organisational assets in their possession at the conclusion of their employment, contract, or agreement.
guidance: Formalising the return process reduces the risk of assets being retained inadvertently or intentionally. Checklist-based exit procedures provide an auditable mechanism for confirming that all items — physical and digital — have been accounted for.

name: Classification of information
description: Calls for information to be classified according to its legal standing, business value, criticality, and the consequences of unauthorised disclosure or alteration.
guidance: Classification decisions should reflect both the organisation's need to share information and any legal or regulatory restrictions on doing so. The resulting scheme serves as the foundation for consistent labelling and handling controls across the asset lifecycle.

name: Labelling of information
description: Requires the development and implementation of labelling procedures that consistently apply classification markings to information assets across all media formats.
guidance: Labelling procedures must extend equally to physical documents and digital assets. Consistent marking enables personnel to recognise the sensitivity level of information at a glance and apply the correct handling controls without ambiguity.

name: Handling of assets
description: Establishes that handling procedures for assets must be developed and put into practice in a manner aligned with the organisation's adopted classification scheme.
guidance: Each classification tier should have its own documented handling instructions covering storage, transmission, copying, access, and disposal. Alignment between classification and handling reduces the risk of accidental mishandling due to unclear guidance.

name: Management of removable media
description: Mandates the implementation of documented procedures for controlling removable media throughout its lifecycle, consistent with the organisation's information classification scheme.
guidance: Removable media governance should include guidance on safeguarding media carrying sensitive content during transit, and clearly defined procedures for the irreversible destruction of media when it is no longer operationally necessary.

name: Disposal of media
description: Requires that media no longer needed be disposed of through a structured, documented process that prevents residual information from being recovered by unauthorised parties.
guidance: Formal disposal procedures reduce the likelihood of sensitive information leakage through discarded or resold media. The method of disposal should be proportionate to the sensitivity of the information previously stored, ranging from secure deletion to physical destruction.

name: Physical media transfer
description: Addresses the need to protect information-bearing media against unauthorised access, tampering, and physical damage while in transit.
guidance: Where confidentiality and integrity are material concerns, protective measures during transportation should be considered — these may include sealed and tamper-evident packaging, use of locked containers, courier-of-hand delivery, or cryptographic protection of the data itself.

name: Access control policy
description: Obliges the organisation to establish, document, and periodically review an access control policy grounded in business and information security requirements.
guidance: The policy should articulate access entitlements by user type or group, covering both logical and physical access dimensions. Regular review keeps entitlements aligned with organisational changes and ensures the policy remains an accurate reflection of intended access boundaries.

name: Access to networks and network services
description: Establishes that network and network service access must be restricted to users who have received explicit authorisation for each specific resource they need.
guidance: A governing policy should enumerate which networks and services are available, to whom, and under what conditions. Access entitlements should be provisioned in accordance with the principle of least privilege and reviewed when roles change.

name: User registration and de-registration
description: Calls for a documented process governing the creation and removal of user accounts, providing the formal foundation for granting and withdrawing access rights.
guidance: The account lifecycle process should enforce unique identifiers for each user, verify that access authorisation exists before provisioning, and ensure that accounts are promptly deactivated or removed when no longer required. Timeliness of de-registration is a common audit focus.

name: User access provisioning
description: Requires a formal provisioning mechanism for assigning or revoking access rights across all user categories, systems, and services.
guidance: Provisioning should include documented authorisation by the relevant information system or service owner, and a check that the access level being granted matches the business need. Evidence of this authorisation trail is typically sought during audits.

name: Management of privileged access rights
description: Mandates strict limitation and oversight of elevated access rights to prevent their misuse or inappropriate accumulation.
guidance: A documented authorisation procedure for privileged access should be maintained, along with a current record of all privileged accounts that have been granted. Sharing of privileged credentials between individuals must be prohibited to preserve individual accountability.

name: Management of secret authentication information of users
description: Establishes that the distribution and ongoing management of secret authentication credentials must be subject to a controlled, formalised process.
guidance: Users should be bound — ideally through a written acknowledgement — to keep their authentication information confidential and to use it exclusively for their own access. The process should include controls to prevent credentials being issued to unintended recipients.

name: Review of user access rights
description: Obliges asset owners to carry out periodic reviews of the access entitlements held by users to confirm they remain appropriate.
guidance: Reviews should be triggered not only on a scheduled basis but also by employment changes such as role transitions, promotions, or departures. Entitlements that are no longer justified should be adjusted or removed promptly following the review.

name: Removal or adjustment of access rights
description: Requires that access entitlements for employees and third-party users be removed or adjusted in a timely manner when their employment, contract, or agreement ends or their role changes.
guidance: Revocation of access should be synchronised with the effective date of termination or role change rather than deferred until a convenient moment. Residual access after departure is a high-frequency finding in ISMS audits.

name: Use of secret authentication information
description: Calls for users to observe the organisation's established practices when using secret authentication information.
guidance: Users should be guided to safeguard their credentials, refrain from recording them in unsecured formats, and report or change credentials immediately upon any suspicion that they have been compromised. These expectations are typically reinforced through awareness training.

name: Information access restriction
description: Establishes that access to information and application functionality must be limited in line with the access control policy.
guidance: Restrictions should be determined by the specific requirements of each application and the sensitivity of the underlying information. Access control settings in applications should be reviewed to confirm they enforce the organisation's intended entitlement boundaries.

name: Secure log-on procedures
description: Addresses the need to gate system and application access through secure log-on procedures wherever the access control policy requires it.
guidance: A well-designed log-on procedure avoids disclosing system or application identifiers before authentication is complete, and presents a general-purpose notice confirming that access is restricted to authorised users. This reduces information leakage and sets a clear accountability baseline.

name: Password management system
description: Mandates that password management tooling be interactive and capable of enforcing credential quality standards.
guidance: Effective systems enforce individual accountability by linking credentials to unique user identities, allow users to select and update their own passwords, and include confirmation steps to catch entry errors. Auditors expect evidence that weak or default credentials cannot persist in production environments.

name: Use of privileged utility programs
description: Requires that utility software capable of bypassing or overriding system and application controls be subject to tight restrictions on availability and use.
guidance: Governance should encompass identification of all relevant utility programs, formal justification and authorisation before use, restriction to the smallest practicable number of trusted individuals, and monitoring of when and how such tools are used.

name: Access control to program source code
description: Establishes that access to program source code must be tightly controlled to prevent unauthorised modification or introduction of unapproved functionality.
guidance: Controls should extend beyond code files to encompass associated artefacts such as design specifications, test plans, and validation documentation. Limiting and logging access to these assets guards against both deliberate tampering and inadvertent changes that could introduce vulnerabilities.

name: Policy on the use of cryptographic controls
description: Calls for the organisation to develop and put into effect a policy governing the application of cryptographic controls for information protection.
guidance: The policy should set out the management approach to cryptography across the organisation, including the circumstances under which encryption is mandatory, the types of algorithm deemed acceptable, and the principles for protecting information at rest and in transit. Classification level is a common driver for determining when cryptographic protection is required.

name: Key management
description: Requires a policy covering the entire lifecycle of cryptographic keys — from generation through storage, distribution, revocation, and retirement — to be developed and put into practice.
guidance: Sound key management is what makes cryptographic protection meaningful; poorly governed keys can neutralise strong encryption entirely. The policy should address key generation, storage media, custody, rotation schedules, and what happens when a key is compromised or reaches end of life.

name: Physical security perimeter
description: Obliges the organization to define and maintain physical boundaries around areas housing sensitive information or critical processing infrastructure.
guidance: Auditors typically check that perimeter definitions are documented and that entry controls are commensurate with the sensitivity of what is enclosed. A perimeter may take many forms — from reinforced walls to staffed reception points — and the choice of barrier should reflect the risk profile of the enclosed area.

name: Physical entry controls
description: Establishes that controlled entry mechanisms must be deployed across secure areas to ensure that only personnel with verified authorisation can gain physical access.
guidance: Controls should include logging of visitor entry and departure times; visitors should receive access only for defined purposes and must be briefed on security requirements and emergency procedures applicable to the area they are entering.

name: Securing offices, rooms and facilities
description: Mandates the deliberate design and consistent application of physical security measures for offices, rooms, and facilities.
guidance: Facility layout and positioning should factor in the potential for uninvited public access. Protective measures should be calibrated to the classification of the information assets housed within each space, with higher-sensitivity areas warranting stronger physical controls.

name: Protecting against external and environmental threats
description: Requires that physical safeguards addressing natural disasters, deliberate attacks, and accidental damage be both designed and put into operation.
guidance: Specialist expertise should be drawn upon when assessing exposure to hazards such as fire, flooding, seismic events, explosion, and civil disturbance. Protective design should reflect the specific risks relevant to each site's geography and operational context.

name: Working in secure areas
description: Calls for operating procedures governing behaviour within secure areas to be formally documented and consistently applied.
guidance: Procedures should cover all individuals working in such spaces, including third-party support personnel. Awareness of a secure area's existence should be confined to those with a legitimate need to know, and solo working within the area should be subject to specific controls or avoided where practicable.

name: Delivery and loading areas
description: Addresses the need to control and, where feasible, isolate delivery and loading areas and other potential entry points to prevent unauthorised access to the premises.
guidance: Inbound access to loading and delivery zones from outside the building should be limited to personnel whose identity and purpose have been confirmed and authorised. Physical separation between these areas and information processing facilities reduces the risk of opportunistic or deliberate intrusion.

name: Equipment siting and protection
description: Requires that equipment be positioned and physically protected so as to reduce exposure to environmental hazards and limit opportunities for unauthorised access.
guidance: Equipment placement should minimise casual passage through work areas containing sensitive assets. Items with elevated risk profiles should be isolated to contain the protection requirements to a smaller footprint, and specific safeguards should address the physical threat vectors relevant to each location.

name: Supporting utilities
description: Establishes that processing equipment must be shielded from power failures and other disruptions originating from failures in infrastructure services.
guidance: Infrastructure services — including electrical supply, water, drainage, heating, ventilation, and cooling — should provide adequate capacity for the systems they underpin. Regular inspection and testing regimes confirm continued serviceability and surface degradation before it causes disruption.

name: Cabling security
description: Mandates protection of power and telecommunications cabling that carries data or supports information services against interception, interference, and physical damage.
guidance: Protective measures include routing cables through conduit and avoiding public or high-traffic areas, implementing protected distribution systems, maintaining physical separation between power and data cabling runs, and applying electromagnetic shielding where warranted.

name: Equipment maintenance
description: Calls for equipment to be serviced and maintained at appropriate intervals to sustain its availability and physical integrity.
guidance: Maintenance should follow vendor-specified schedules and technical requirements. Only personnel with verified authorisation should conduct repairs or servicing. Records of maintenance activity provide an audit trail and support accountability for the condition of critical equipment.

name: Removal of assets
description: Obliges the organisation to require prior authorisation before any equipment, information, or software is removed from organisational premises.
guidance: Removal should only proceed following documented approval. A register of all assets currently held off-site provides visibility over organisational property in external environments and facilitates recovery or accountability in the event of loss.

name: Security of equipment and assets off-premises
description: Establishes that security controls must be extended to assets used or stored outside organisational premises, accounting for the heightened risk profile of external environments.
guidance: Exposure to unauthorised access in public or shared locations should inform risk treatment decisions for off-premises equipment. Assets must be kept under continuous protective measures to guard against theft, loss, and environmental damage regardless of location.

name: Secure disposal or reuse of equipment
description: Requires verification that all equipment containing storage media has been cleared of sensitive data and licensed software through approved methods before being disposed of or repurposed.
guidance: Where storage devices hold sensitive content, physical destruction may be the most reliable disposal method; alternatively, data may be rendered unrecoverable through approved overwriting or degaussing techniques. Standard delete operations are insufficient and should not be relied upon.

name: Unattended user equipment
description: Calls for users to take responsibility for ensuring that equipment left unattended is protected in accordance with organisational security requirements.
guidance: Personnel should be made aware of what constitutes adequate protection for unattended equipment, the procedures to follow before leaving a workstation, and their individual accountability for applying those measures consistently.

name: Clear desk and clear screen policy
description: Mandates the adoption of a clear desk practice for physical documents and removable storage media, and a clear screen practice for information processing equipment.
guidance: The scope and stringency of these practices should be shaped by the information classification scheme, relevant legal and contractual duties, the organisation's risk assessments, and the cultural context of the working environment. Auditors commonly check for posted reminders and evidence of compliance during walkthroughs.

name: Documented operating procedures
description: Requires that procedures for information processing activities are written down and kept accessible to the personnel responsible for carrying them out.
guidance: Procedures covering operational activities tied to processing and communication facilities should be authored, version-controlled, and subject to formal management authorization before any update takes effect. Auditors look for evidence that staff can locate and act on current procedures without reliance on informal knowledge.

name: Change management
description: Calls for the organization to maintain formal governance over changes that touch organizational structure, business processes, information processing facilities, or systems wherever those changes carry implications for information security.
guidance: Before changes are executed, re-assessment of security risk exposure is warranted, alongside identification of all internal and external stakeholders who need to be notified. Timing constraints — such as avoiding changes during peak business periods — should be built into the change control process.

name: Capacity management
description: Addresses the need to track resource utilization, adjust system performance where necessary, and project future capacity so that systems continue to meet operational demands.
guidance: Capacity planning should factor in the business criticality of each affected system. Ongoing tuning and performance monitoring serve both availability and efficiency objectives; where shortfalls are identified, corrective action should be taken before service levels are compromised.

name: Separation of development, testing and operational environments
description: Mandates that development, testing, and live operational environments are kept distinct from one another in order to limit exposure to unauthorized access or inadvertent modification of production systems.
guidance: The depth of isolation required between each environment should be assessed based on the risk of operational disruption. Once the necessary degree of separation is established, appropriate controls should be put in place and validated to confirm they achieve the intended protection.

name: Controls against malware
description: Obliges the organization to put in place detection, prevention, and recovery measures against malware, supplemented by a program to keep users informed of related threats.
guidance: A layered approach combining technical detection and repair tools with access and change management controls, alongside ongoing user awareness, provides more robust protection than any single measure. Auditors look for evidence that the malware protection strategy is reviewed as the threat landscape evolves.

name: Information backup
description: Establishes that backup copies of information, software, and system images must be produced and periodically tested in line with a documented backup policy agreed by the organization.
guidance: The scope and frequency of backup activity should reflect the criticality of the data, its sensitivity, and how central it is to continued operations. Both full and differential approaches may be appropriate depending on recovery time objectives; testing restores — not just the backup process itself — confirms recoverability when it matters.

name: Event logging
description: Requires the organization to generate, retain, and periodically examine event logs that capture user activities, system exceptions, faults, and information security-relevant occurrences.
guidance: Effective log records include user identifiers, timestamps, system activity details, privilege usage, file access entries, and network connection data. Regular review of logs is as important as their collection; without review, logs provide accountability in hindsight but no timely detection value.

name: Protection of log information
description: Calls for logging infrastructure and the records it produces to be safeguarded against unauthorized access and any form of tampering or deletion.
guidance: Controls should be directed at preventing alterations to log files, whether through unauthorized editing, deletion, or overwriting of the underlying storage. Where logs are used to support investigations or demonstrate compliance, their integrity must be provable to an independent reviewer.

name: Administrator and operator logs
description: Addresses the need to capture and retain records of actions taken by system administrators and operators, with those records protected and subject to periodic examination.
guidance: Privileged-user activity logs serve as both a deterrent and a detection mechanism. Because administrators often have the ability to modify logs, storage arrangements should prevent self-modification; regular independent review is the primary control for identifying misuse or anomalies.

name: Clock synchronisation
description: Mandates that all relevant information processing systems within an organization or security domain have their clocks aligned to a single authoritative time reference.
guidance: Accurate and consistent timestamps across systems are essential for correlating log entries during investigations and for satisfying evidentiary requirements in legal or disciplinary proceedings. Drift in system clocks can render log-based timelines unreliable, undermining their usefulness as evidence.

name: Installation of software on operational systems
description: Calls for formal procedures governing which software may be installed on systems in live operational use, so that only authorized code reaches the production environment.
guidance: To protect the integrity of operational systems, procedures should permit only validated executable code to be deployed; development code, compilers, and unapproved utilities should be excluded. Auditors expect to see evidence of authorization records for software installations alongside enforcement mechanisms such as allowlisting or privileged-account controls.

name: Management of technical vulnerabilities
description: Requires the organization to obtain timely intelligence on technical vulnerabilities affecting its information systems, assess the resulting exposure, and take proportionate action to address the associated risk.
guidance: Maintaining a current and complete picture of vulnerability status across systems in use is a prerequisite for effective risk management. Timely patching or other mitigating measures for known vulnerabilities reduces the window of opportunity for exploitation; the organization should have a defined process for prioritizing remediation based on assessed risk.

name: Restrictions on software installation
description: Establishes that the organization must define and put into practice rules governing what software users are permitted to install on organizational systems.
guidance: A clear policy setting out permitted and prohibited software categories, enforced through technical controls such as application allowlisting or privilege restrictions, limits the risk of unauthorized or malicious software reaching operational systems. The principle of least privilege should guide how installation rights are assigned.

name: Information systems audit controls
description: Addresses the need to plan and coordinate audit activities that involve checking live operational systems, so that business operations experience minimal disruption.
guidance: Audit requirements and the technical scope of verification activities should be agreed with management in advance. Careful scheduling and scoping help ensure that audit work gathers the evidence needed without triggering unintended service impacts on production environments.

name: Network controls
description: Obliges the organization to actively manage and control its networks in order to protect information held in connected systems and applications.
guidance: Those responsible for network management should implement measures ensuring that information in transit is protected and that connected services are accessible only to authorized parties. Routing controls deserve particular attention, as misconfigured routes can allow connections or data flows that contravene the organization's access control policy.

name: Security of network services
description: Calls for the security characteristics, service levels, and management obligations of every network service to be identified and captured in formal agreements with service providers, regardless of whether those services are delivered internally or by a third party.
guidance: Security features and management requirements for network services should be documented before agreements are finalized. Providers — whether in-house teams or external suppliers — should demonstrate the capability to deliver and maintain services in a manner consistent with the agreed security requirements.

name: Segregation in networks
description: Requires that distinct groups of information services, users, and information systems are kept separate at the network level.
guidance: Dividing large networks into discrete domains is a practical means of containing the impact of a security compromise and applying differentiated controls. Domains may be delineated on the basis of trust levels, system criticality, or the sensitivity classification of the information processed within them.

name: Information transfer policies and procedures
description: Establishes that formal policies, procedures, and controls governing the transfer of information must be in place and must cover all types of communication channel used by the organization.
guidance: Procedures for electronic information transfer should address the risk of messages being intercepted, copied, altered, or removed during transit. Controls should be matched to the sensitivity of the information being transmitted and the characteristics of the communication channel in use.

name: Agreements on information transfer
description: Requires formal agreements to be in place with external parties that address how business information will be securely exchanged between those parties and the organization.
guidance: The level of protection specified in transfer agreements should correspond to the sensitivity of the information being shared. Agreements serve as the enforceable basis for the security obligations of all parties involved in the exchange.

name: Electronic messaging
description: Calls for appropriate protective measures to be applied to information conveyed through electronic messaging systems.
guidance: Security considerations for electronic messaging encompass protection against unauthorized access to message content, prevention of unauthorized modification, assurance that messages reach their intended recipients reliably, and maintenance of service availability. The specific controls selected should reflect the sensitivity of the information transmitted and the risk profile of the messaging platform.

name: Confidentiality or non-disclosure agreements
description: Mandates that the organization identify, document, and keep current its requirements for confidentiality or non-disclosure agreements that reflect the need to protect information shared with or by external parties, and that such agreements undergo periodic review.
guidance: Confidentiality agreements should employ legally enforceable terms proportionate to the sensitivity of the information concerned. Where external parties are given access to confidential organizational information, agreements must clearly define obligations, the consequences of breach, and the arrangements for return or destruction of information when the relationship ends.

name: Information security requirements analysis and specification
description: Requires that information security considerations form part of the stated requirements whenever new information systems are acquired or built, or when existing systems are significantly enhanced.
guidance: Security requirements apply across all system types — infrastructure components, business applications, and commercial off-the-shelf products alike. Framing requirements in testable terms from the outset makes it possible to verify during development and acceptance that those requirements have been met.

name: Securing application services on public networks
description: Calls for information transmitted via application services operating over public networks to be safeguarded against fraudulent activity, unauthorized disclosure, unauthorized modification, and disputes over contractual obligations.
guidance: Applications exposed to public networks face a distinct set of threats — including incomplete or misdirected transmissions, unauthorized message alteration, message replay, and repudiation — that require specific technical controls. The selection of controls should be guided by the sensitivity of the data and the commercial or legal consequences of compromise.

name: Protecting application services transactions
description: Addresses the need to protect information involved in application service transactions against incomplete transmission, misdirection, unauthorized alteration, unauthorized disclosure, duplication, and replay attacks.
guidance: Suitable controls for transaction integrity include cryptographic techniques, use of secure communication protocols, and access restrictions limited to authorized users. The combination of controls should be proportionate to the value and sensitivity of the transactions being protected.

name: Secure development policy
description: Calls for the organization to define and enforce security rules governing internal software and system development, with those rules applied consistently across all development activity.
guidance: A secure development policy typically encompasses security requirements within development methodologies, coding standards, developer competency in security topics, the capacity to verify security properties, and appropriate configuration of the development environment. Auditors look for evidence that the policy is applied consistently across projects rather than treated as optional guidance.

name: System change control procedures
description: Requires that modifications to systems throughout the development lifecycle are governed by formal change control procedures to prevent unauthorized or accidental corruption of system integrity.
guidance: Documented change control procedures should address formal request recording, impact assessment, test planning, and rollback arrangements. Their consistent application reduces the risk of untested or undocumented changes reaching operational systems and provides an audit trail that supports post-incident analysis.

name: Technical review of applications after operating platform changes
description: Establishes that business-critical applications must be reviewed and tested following any change to an underlying operating platform, to confirm that the change has not introduced adverse effects on operations or security.
guidance: The review should incorporate an assessment of how platform changes affect existing security controls and integrity mechanisms. Where gaps are identified, compensating controls or remedial actions should be defined and implemented before the changed platform is accepted into live use.

name: Restrictions on changes to software packages
description: Addresses the need to discourage modifications to vendor-supplied software packages and, where changes are unavoidable, to limit them to what is strictly necessary and subject each change to rigorous control.
guidance: Where possible, software packages should be used without alteration to preserve vendor-supported functionality and simplify future upgrades. Any deviation from this position requires documented justification, formal authorization, and controls to manage the resulting maintenance burden and risk of introducing security weaknesses.

name: Secure system engineering principles
description: Obliges the organization to establish, document, and maintain secure engineering principles, and to apply them throughout every system implementation effort.
guidance: Secure engineering principles span all architectural layers — business, data, application, and technology — and encompass structured security design techniques and security control engineering methods. Embedding these principles as standard practice, rather than applying them retrospectively, is more effective and less costly for the organization.

name: Secure development environment
description: Requires organizations to create and maintain protected development environments covering the full development and integration lifecycle, accounting for all people, processes, and technology involved.
guidance: Securing the development environment involves controlling physical and logical access, monitoring change activity, and separating development resources from production systems. The sensitivity of systems under development should influence the rigor of the controls applied to the environment.

name: Outsourced development
description: Calls for the organization to oversee and keep track of all system development work delegated to external parties.
guidance: Where software development is carried out by third parties, the organization should verify that outsourced activities align with its own security policies and contractual obligations. Oversight mechanisms may include contractual audit rights, progress reporting, code reviews, or regular security assessments of the supplier's development practices.

name: System security testing
description: Addresses the requirement to conduct security-focused testing of system functionality as a normal part of the development process.
guidance: Security testing during development should confirm that security controls have been implemented correctly and that they operate as intended under realistic conditions. Testing should be treated as integral to the development lifecycle rather than a late-stage gate, enabling defects to be caught and remediated before release.

name: System acceptance testing
description: Requires that formal acceptance testing programs and associated pass/fail criteria are defined for new information systems, major upgrades, and new releases before those systems are put into production.
guidance: Acceptance criteria should be established early and agreed before testing begins, ensuring that testers and stakeholders share a common understanding of what constitutes a satisfactory outcome. Test documentation provides evidence of due diligence and supports post-deployment accountability if security deficiencies are later discovered.

name: Protection of test data
description: Mandates that test data be chosen with care and that it is properly safeguarded and managed throughout the testing lifecycle.
guidance: Operational or production data should be avoided in test environments wherever feasible. If live data is used for testing purposes, it should be anonymized or de-identified before use, and any sensitive data introduced into the test environment should be securely removed once testing is concluded.

name: Information security policy for supplier relationships
description: Requires that the organization agree on and formally document information security requirements with each supplier before granting that supplier access to organizational assets, ensuring that access-related risks are addressed from the outset.
guidance: Requirements may be captured in a dedicated supplier security policy or in supplier-type-specific agreements. Auditors look for documentation that suppliers understand their obligations and that the organization has a process for ensuring adherence rather than relying solely on contractual language.

name: Addressing security within supplier agreements
description: Calls for every supplier that may access, process, store, transmit, or provide IT infrastructure components for the organization to be covered by a formal agreement that sets out all relevant security obligations.
guidance: Supplier agreements should be calibrated to the level of access granted, identifying which categories of information the supplier may handle, the minimum security standards required, and the procedures to follow in the event of a data breach or security incident affecting organizational information.

name: Information and communication technology supply chain
description: Establishes that supplier agreements must specifically address information security risks inherent in ICT product and service supply chains, not merely the direct supplier relationship.
guidance: Supply chain security provisions should require that suppliers extend appropriate security practices to any sub-suppliers they engage. Where components or services pass through multiple tiers before reaching the organization, the agreement should give the organization visibility into — and recourse over — security practices further down the chain.

name: Monitoring and review of supplier services
description: Obliges organizations to conduct ongoing monitoring, periodic review, and audit of the services delivered by their suppliers to confirm that contractual and security obligations are being met.
guidance: Supplier monitoring activities should verify compliance with agreed security terms and ensure that any security incidents or service problems are being handled appropriately. Findings from monitoring should feed into supplier relationship management and, where necessary, trigger contract review or remediation discussions.

name: Managing changes to supplier services
description: Addresses the need to manage changes in supplier service delivery — including updates to policies, procedures, and controls — in a way that accounts for the criticality of the business information and processes involved, and that includes re-evaluation of associated risks.
guidance: When supplier changes affect systems or processes of high business criticality, the organization should assess the implications for existing security controls before accepting the change. Where gaps or new risks are identified, policies and procedures should be updated accordingly.

name: Responsibilities and procedures
description: Requires management to establish defined responsibilities and documented procedures that enable the organization to respond to information security incidents promptly, systematically, and effectively.
guidance: Incident management procedures should identify the categories of events and incidents likely to arise, clarify who is responsible for detection, reporting, and response, and define the escalation and reporting chains — including the level of management authority needed to authorize specific response actions.

name: Reporting information security events
description: Establishes that identified information security events must be escalated through defined management channels without undue delay, enabling timely response and containment.
guidance: A formal event reporting procedure should exist alongside an escalation process that specifies actions to be taken on receipt of a report. Clear reporting channels reduce the likelihood that events go unacknowledged and help preserve the timeliness of response that is critical to limiting impact.

name: Reporting information security weaknesses
description: Calls for all employees and contractors who use the organization's systems and services to be obliged to observe and report any suspected or confirmed information security weaknesses.
guidance: Effective weakness reporting depends on personnel understanding their obligation and having a straightforward, accessible mechanism to make reports. Prompt escalation of identified weaknesses enables the organization to investigate and address them before they develop into incidents.

name: Assessment of and decision on information security events
description: Mandates a triage process under which reported information security events are evaluated and a determination made as to whether each qualifies for classification as a security incident warranting a formal response.
guidance: A designated triage function or individual should be responsible for assessing events against defined classification criteria. A consistent classification process ensures that genuine incidents receive appropriate priority while routine anomalies are handled proportionately, avoiding both under-response and resource waste.

name: Response to information security incidents
description: Requires that the organization respond to confirmed information security incidents by following its documented incident response procedures.
guidance: Early preservation of evidence is an important consideration once an incident is confirmed, as the window for capturing forensically useful data can be short. Formal response procedures should account for evidence collection, containment, eradication, and recovery steps, and should designate responsibility for each phase.

name: Learning from information security incidents
description: Addresses the need to apply insights gained through the analysis and resolution of past information security incidents to reduce the probability or severity of future occurrences.
guidance: Mechanisms for capturing the nature, frequency, and cost of incidents enable the organization to identify patterns — such as recurring incident types or high-impact categories — and target preventive action where it will be most effective. Post-incident reviews should translate findings into concrete improvements to controls or procedures.

name: Collection of evidence
description: Obliges the organization to define and follow procedures for identifying, collecting, acquiring, and preserving material that may serve as evidence in relation to security incidents.
guidance: Evidence handling procedures should maintain a demonstrable chain of custody to support potential disciplinary or legal action. Internally developed procedures should address how evidence is collected from different source types, how its integrity is maintained, and how it is stored to prevent contamination or unauthorized access.

name: Planning information security continuity
theme: Information security aspects of business continuity management
description: Requires the organization to assess what level of information security capability must be maintained during crisis or disaster scenarios, and to use those requirements as the basis for continuity planning.
guidance: The organization should determine whether information security continuity is integrated within broader business continuity management or governed separately. Regardless of the approach chosen, a designated ownership structure for continuity management should be in place to ensure accountability for both planning and response.

name: Implementing information security continuity
theme: Information security aspects of business continuity management
description: Calls for the organization to establish, document, put into practice, and sustain the processes, procedures, and controls needed to preserve the required security posture when normal operating conditions are disrupted.
guidance: Continuity plans should specifically address how security controls will be maintained — or supplemented — under adverse conditions, given that controls effective in normal operation may not function as expected during an emergency. Gaps between normal-state and emergency-state security capabilities should be identified and addressed in planning.

name: Verify, review and evaluate information security continuity
theme: Information security aspects of business continuity management
description: Establishes that the organization must periodically verify its information security continuity controls to confirm they remain valid and will operate effectively if an adverse situation arises.
guidance: Verification activities should test not only whether controls function under simulated adverse conditions but also whether changes to processes, technology, or personnel since the last review have affected their adequacy. Where gaps or obsolescence are found, controls should be updated before the next adverse event occurs.

name: Availability of information processing facilities
theme: Information security aspects of business continuity management
description: Mandates that information processing facilities are provisioned with adequate redundancy to satisfy the organization's defined availability requirements.
guidance: Redundancy can be achieved through duplication of individual components, architectural redundancy, or a combination of both. Failover from primary to redundant components must be tested to verify that it operates correctly under realistic conditions; untested redundancy provides no reliable assurance of availability.

name: Identification of applicable legislation and contractual requirements
description: Obliges the organization to explicitly identify and maintain a current record of all statutory, regulatory, and contractual obligations that apply to each information system and to the organization as a whole, along with a documented account of how it intends to meet each requirement.
guidance: Legal obligations can differ across jurisdictions and may be affected by where information is created, stored, or transmitted. For each applicable requirement, specific controls and individual responsibilities should be defined and documented so that compliance can be demonstrated to auditors and regulators.

name: Intellectual property rights
description: Calls for the organization to put appropriate procedures in place to meet its legislative, regulatory, and contractual obligations concerning intellectual property rights and the use of proprietary software.
guidance: An intellectual property compliance policy that articulates rules for the lawful acquisition and use of software and information products provides a clear baseline for staff. Acquiring software only through reputable, authorized channels and maintaining evidence of licenses supports compliance and reduces legal exposure.

name: Protection of records
description: Requires that organizational records be protected against loss, destruction, falsification, unauthorized access, and unauthorized disclosure, in a manner consistent with applicable legal, regulatory, contractual, and business requirements.
guidance: Certain records carry particular evidential or statutory significance — for example, those demonstrating that the ISMS has operated in conformance with its requirements. Retention schedules, access controls, and integrity protections should be calibrated to the regulatory and business importance of each record category.

name: Privacy and protection of personally identifiable information
description: Addresses the obligation to protect personally identifiable information and uphold privacy rights in accordance with relevant legislation and regulation, where applicable.
guidance: A data protection and privacy policy that defines how personal information is collected, processed, stored, and disposed of provides the governance foundation for this control. The policy should be communicated to all personnel who handle personal data, and compliance should be verifiable through documented practices.

name: Regulation of cryptographic controls
description: Mandates that the organization's use of cryptographic controls conforms to all relevant legal agreements, legislation, and regulations.
guidance: The legal treatment of cryptography varies by jurisdiction, and controls that are permissible in one country may be restricted or prohibited in another. Where encrypted information or cryptographic tools cross national boundaries, legal advice on the applicable import and export rules should be obtained before implementation.

name: Independent review of information security
description: Requires that the organization's overall approach to managing information security — covering objectives, controls, policies, processes, and procedures — undergoes independent review at planned intervals or when material changes occur.
guidance: Management should determine the scope, frequency, and conduct of independent reviews, which may be carried out through internal audit functions or by a designated external or internal body with sufficient independence. The objective is to provide assurance that the information security management approach remains fit for purpose and is being implemented as intended.

name: Compliance with security policies and standards
description: Calls for managers to conduct periodic checks confirming that information processing activities and associated procedures within their areas of responsibility comply with applicable security policies, standards, and other security requirements.
guidance: Where a review uncovers non-conformance, managers should document the finding together with its root cause, determine what corrective action is needed to prevent recurrence, implement that action, and verify that it has been effective. This cycle provides a feedback loop that strengthens the organization's overall compliance posture.

name: Technical compliance review
description: Requires information systems to be periodically checked against the organization's information security policies and standards to confirm technical compliance.
guidance: Automated tooling capable of generating technical compliance reports is the preferred approach where available, as it enables broader coverage and more consistent assessment than manual methods. Where automated tools are not available, technically qualified personnel should perform the review. Results should be interpreted by someone with the expertise to distinguish genuine findings from false positives.
